creator: wbe@psr.com
text:
[New offer (same as the old offer but with a shiny, new, more recognizable faceplate :)]

"The 2025.1 announcement"

When I saw how FreeBSD gets file(1) from an upstream site, I realized it made more sense to do likewise.  Doing so gives FreeBSD the option of making it just a contributed software port, adopting it as /usr/bin/calendar, ignoring it completely, or whatever.  As forks, I get the freedom to convert everything to my coding style, make improvements, fix bugs, make changes to the calendars collection, etc.

I previously called both these forks "version 2025.1", but the more I saw "2025.1", the less I liked it, so I changed it, for both calendar(1) and what's currently deskutils/calendar-data, to version numbers that are like their current version numbers.

If you're interested, see "http://www.psr.com/calendars.html".

What's there:
  a summary
  calendar-2.0.0   (= major.minor.patch level)
  calendars-2025.2 (named like calendar-data 2022.11 from Nov. 2022).

calendars-2025.2 includes the changes and suggestions from bug 157748.

Enjoy,
 -WBE
----------
Historical note of little significance to anyone but me:

I now consider the first tarball (formerly 2024.1) to be version 1.0.0, the two patches (formerly 2024.2 and .3) to be 1.0.1 and 1.0.2, and 2024.4 to be 1.1.0.  "calendar 2025.1" is a big enough change that it's now 2.0.0, and, no, I don't worry about running out of positive integers. :)

The number gap is large enough that there's plenty of room in case you want to have 0.9.4, 0.10.1, etc.
